explicit_text: I was born in 1899 in a turpentine camp near Eastman, Georgia, where my father was the manager of a 20-crop naval stores place. Growing up there, I was surrounded by the turpentine business from the start, living right in the camp with my family. Eastman was where my early memories of turpentine work began, and it shaped my entire career.

That Georgia town was where my early memories of turpentine work began, and it shaped my entire career.

explicit_text: When I was about two years old, my family moved from Georgia to another turpentine camp at Bay Lake, Florida. It was there I started school at six in a small one-room log schoolhouse in the woods. Bay Lake was my first Florida home and the place where I began my schooling while still connected to the turpentine camps.

explicit_text: Around the time I was ten, we moved to a turpentine camp at Martin, Florida, which was about seven miles from Ocala. There, I was promoted to tally man, keeping track of the work done by the laborers. Martin was important in my early career because it was where I learned to manage and inspect the work in the woods.

Ocala was both a place of education and a base for some of my early business ventures.

explicit_text: In about 1914, when I was 15, we moved to Loraine in Manatee County, Florida, about 12 miles from Bradenton. There, I worked as a tally man and inspector of box men, honing my skills as an expert box cutter. Loraine was a stepping stone in my turpentine career before I moved on to other camps and roles.

explicit_text: I worked as a manager and woodsman at a 30-crop turpentine place in Verna, Florida, for Mr. Hamp Lowther before joining the army in 1917. This job involved managing the commissary and riding one ride in the woods. Verna was one of the last camps I worked at before my military service in World War I.

explicit_text: I joined the United States Army in 1917 and served during World War I, going overseas to France. explicit_text: In 1920, I worked as an over-rider over eight woodsmen on a 100-crop turpentine job at Nalaca, Florida. explicit_text: In 1922, I was offered a better job managing eight camps owned by a New York company at Opal in Okeechobee County. This was a large virgin woods operation with 120 crops, and I managed 400 laborers and nine woodsmen. Opal was a challenging but rewarding position until a terrible rainy season forced me to quit.

explicit_text: By late 1924, I had saved some money and bought a 10-crop turpentine place and 200 acres of farmland at Spring Park in Harlon County. Unfortunately, the Florida land boom drew away my laborers to cities and northern states, making it impossible to run the place profitably. Spring Park was my attempt at ownership, but it didn't work out due to economic conditions.

explicit_text: I heard a story in 1916 from J. A. Stevens, a foreman of a turpentine camp in Manatee County, about how he had to arrange a mass legal marriage for about fifty Negro couples. This was after county officials ordered the couples to be legally married or stop living together. Stevens' experience illustrated the unique social customs and camp governance challenges in the turpentine camps.

explicit_text: While I mentioned Polk County and other nearby areas, Bartow, Florida, was the county seat where courthouse marriages could be performed. Couples in the turpentine camps sometimes went to Bartow to get legally married, although most marriages were 'commissary weddings' arranged by foremen. Bartow represented the legal center for formalizing marriages in the region.
